chunks.title: Dosing Recommendations for Liver Supplements
chunks.summary: The discussion starts with how to approach dosing for liver supplements, emphasizing an intuitive method where individuals can gauge their own needs by adjusting the quantity based on their feelings. This part also touches on concerns regarding vitamin A toxicity and the role of liver as a detoxifier, challenging common misconceptions.
chunks.transcript: what what dosing do you give your clients any recommendations on dosing for liver do you let them do it intuitively this comes up all the time and we can both address it from our own perspectives is there a concern for vitamin a toxicity and pregnancy it's a really interesting rabbit hole but i'd love to get your take on it yeah i'm intuitive always and it's kind of interesting when i start with that in my conversations with my clients when i say you know go and if you know if they're eating raw liver or if they're taking the pills like go and see what it is that you need like if you're taking the pill like put three in your hand and see how that feels and then put like four in your hand and see how that feels and then if you feel like you need more later in the day add more and later in the day and that's definitely like not a standard way to make a recommendation for any type of supplement or medication um but i think it takes away our intuitive power so as much as i would love to like fully have medicine step back into that i don't think it will ever happen but if we can start planting those seeds i think it's essential the the vitamin a toxicity is definitely something that comes up and then i also always hear like what about the liver being the filter for all the toxins and does that mean that you know we're getting all the toxins from the liver and um there's just so much misinformation

chunks.title: Understanding Liver Function and Toxicity
chunks.summary: The conversation explains that the liver is not just a filter for toxins but serves as an enzymatic factory that modifies and prepares toxins for excretion. There’s also a discussion about the safety of consuming liver from well-fed animals and debunking myths about liver accumulating toxins, specifically in comparison to kidneys.
chunks.transcript: you addressed the vitamin a and then i don't know if you want to move into the copper part of it too but there's there's this vitamin a copper toxicity thing that just keeps looming i feel like on social media that you know that's just like the number one kind of fear that people have around this yeah it is it is interesting so my perspective on liver is that it's not a filter it's like i think we're both pretty safely in that camp uh i think most people who understand human physiology know that the liver is not a filter it doesn't act like a sieve this is not i i don't think many people listen to this podcast eat pasta or spaghetti but this is not a sieve catching the spaghetti after you've cooked it and your liver isn't just full of toxic spaghetti that it's caught you know and filtered out of your body the liver is a enzymatic factory that biotransforms things so it'll take compounds that are toxins and it'll make them water-soluble or it'll bio-convert them so that you can excrete them or you can bind them and so basically it prepares them for excretion so it takes one widget and it changes the shape of that widget and then it exports it out the back door and that's either poop or pee but the liver doesn't really store those things at all so you're not going to accumulate toxins in the liver having said that the sources of liver is important because if it's a poorly fed cow or a cow from a toxic area of course they're going to have more toxins in the body but the liver generally doesn't hold on to toxins some people would say heavy metals but if you look at it so there's a farm that i love in georgia called white oak pastures and they've sent me their analysis of heavy metals in the organs the kidneys actually store more heavy metals than the liver the liver is pretty low in heavy metals and so i think that i don't the kidneys are not incredibly high but the kidneys are higher than the liver so when i tell people it's you know i'd say eat less kidney than you do liver or eat a marginal or a small amount of kidney can be still valuable i think for a lot of people especially perhaps people with urinary issues or people that want vitamin a in smaller doses the kidney is also a source of dao which is diamine oxidase which is a valuable antihistaminergic thing for people that have sort of histamine problems and they're trying to work those out but um i'm not saying the kidney is toxic just the kidney is even higher in heavy metals in the liver and neither of them are very high in general

chunks.title: Vitamin A Intake During Pregnancy
chunks.summary: The segment focuses on recommended vitamin A intake for pregnant women, suggesting that less than 10,000 IU is adequate and that small amounts of liver are sufficient during pregnancy. Additionally, it discusses the risks of vitamin A deficiency which are far more common than toxicity.
chunks.transcript: especially from a good source like white oak pastures in terms of the vitamin a levels i think that the the recommendations are less than ten thousand i use for a pregnant woman per day which is fine you can stay below ten thousand iu's by eating less than two ounces of liver per day if my if my calculations are correct i think that's right i think an ounce of liver has a little more than 5 000 ius of vitamin a per day so i don't even think pregnant women need that much liver i think i'd be happy if a pregnant woman is getting half an ounce of liver throughout the week or an average of three and a half ounces per week is plenty for most women in terms of the liver you could go a little higher if you wanted to do an ounce a day that's seven ounces per week but i don't think pregnant women need a whole lot more liver than that per week and that's well below those recommendations if you go down the rabbit hole and i've done a few podcasts on that it's questionable whether higher doses of vitamin a would even be problematic for women with uh with their babies and i think that in general it's pretty safe to say that vitamin a deficiency is 10 to 100 to a thousand times more common than a vitamin a toxicity having said that i don't recommend that people eat six pounds of liver or go in

chunks.title: Copper Deficiency and Its Importance
chunks.summary: The speakers delve into the significance of copper, discussing its essential role in various enzymatic functions and the potential deficiency issues, particularly in the context of high zinc intake. They highlight the need for balance in nutrient supplementation to prevent deficiencies that mimic more severe conditions.

chunks.title: Understanding Anemia in Pregnancy
chunks.summary: This segment covers the physiological reasons behind anemia during pregnancy and how it relates to blood dilution. It promotes the use of an animal-based diet, particularly organ meats, for optimal nutrition during pregnancy to counteract the effects of hemodilution and support overall well-being.
